commentBody: Hi Wordplay community—Zhou (co-constructor) here. In honor of Isa (notable rescuer of three-legged cats), Kevin (co-constructor and cat person who won’t let me get a dog) and I are donating the money from this puzzle to the Camano Animal Shelter Association: https://www.camanoanimalshelter.org/donate. Please consider donating to Casa (or to a cause close to your own heart, if three-legged cats aren’t your thing). Thanks for being a bright spot in this wild world.
I totally loved this puzzle. So sorry about Isa -- they sound like such a great friend and a special human being.

This is my favorite kind of theme puzzle: where the revealer/theme helps me complete clues I hadn't gotten to yet.

I am saddened by the loss of the author's neighbor Isa. May their memory be a blessing.
A bright, straightforward puzzle.  As for the theme; IGOTIT!  The puzzle also featured two of my favorite singer songwriters (Tori AMOS and Paul SIMON) and plenty of GOODEATS for an OMNIVORE NOLESS.

Came here to say that while some people might understand the phrase NO LESS to mean equally, I have never interpreted it that way, but instead I understand it to mean "at least as much as".

In other words: exactly what "no less" means — no more, no less.
Ah, the story about Isa was lovely. It’s hard to lose such a wonderful neighbor. On another note, the clue of “pants worn on rainy days” was delightful. I kept thinking of rain gear…doh!
Are PAnAMA pants a thing? Because I’m pretty sure Santa is feeling nO L Y.
Delightful puzzle. No notes.

I thought I was a big fan of Kevin Curry and Zhou Zhang. Now I worship them. Great puzzle, beautiful constructor notes and dedication (so sorry for the loss of your good friend and neighbor Isa), AND they support local animal rescue. I’m pretty sure I’m way older than they are, but I want to be just like them when I grow up.
Japanese crossing French and two names. Ouch!

Remember “Escape Room”? Same pair. Winner of the Sunday Griddies for 2025. And Zhou’s 8/27 puzzle won the M-W category. So they know how to make a great puzzle and this fits the bill. 

I also love the gustatory sub theme: food. If you wonder what to get when shopping at your local agora, how about ham, fava beans, koi in a delicate ponzu sauce, olives, picante chicken, yak, an oat tart with pecan filling, lemonade to drink… Good eats. And while Kevin and Zhou may be omnivores, I’m leaving seals off the menu (they’re cute and they often spend a lot of time AT SEA taking care of their own dietary needs, so I’d prefer that nobody ATE them).
Wha whaaaaaa

Lovely puzzle, lovely story, lovely cause. 

Señor Gato, who’s been zooming from window to window since 4am because birds!!! has just been advised how lucky he is to have all four of his 15-plus-year-old legs.

Folks, before you get your latte this morning, please consider donating to CASA instead. If you go to their Amazon wishlist, you’ll get an idea of what a shoestring budget they operate on.

So before I read the constructor notes I was going to start by saying how excited I always am for a Zhou/Curry collaboration (or solo efforts for that matter)—I always expect a clever twisty theme, a sterling grid, and sparkling clues, and wasn’t disappointed today (uplifting coverage, anyone?). Well, actually this theme is a little tame by their standards, but still tight, fun and entertaining. 

But now after reading their notes I can focus on telling them that if their goal is to bring joy to others with their crosswords, they certainly have accomplished that with me, and I’m sure many others. I’m heartbroken to hear of the passing of their friend, and what a fitting way to pay tribute to someone else who brought joy to others in their own way as well. Thanks for passing it on, over and out!
50% easy and 50% twisty, which is a perfect Wednesday. And thank you for sharing the tribute to your neighbor. Loss is hard.
Sam makes working the puzzles more fun.

An entire chemistry exam about carbon?  Nah.

But the judges will allow it given the overall fun quotient of this light and clever Wednesday.

I comment only when one of Zhou's puzzles publishes, which is a nerdy kind of friendship loyalty. My usual "so fun, Zhou's the best!" isn't quite right this time. Zhou and Kevin are indeed the best, so I'm sure Isa was an absolute treasure. In memory of Isa and in celebration of Kaela 🕯️🎂
Clever puzzle that was deeply and resoundingly enjoyable! Bravo.
Has anyone found the hidden reference to Kaela mentioned  in the constructor notes?

So sorry for your loss.  Crossword puzzles are a wonderful way to connect with others. 

The puzzle you created is a wonderful homage to your friendship and message of universal understanding.
Both hall and oat but not crossing is a shame

If I'm stuck in a certain section, rotating through the clues waiting for inspiration, and I think of something I get an immediate surge of some cool chemical.

(Naturally synthesized brain chemical, I mean, like all of you.  No, I'm not shooting up every time I fill something in.)

And part of that spurt of happy is that I think that's going to unlock the entire problem area I have.  And sometimes it do, and sometimes it don't. 

Tonight it did not, in a pretty substantial way.  

I call this a jungly puzzle--no matter how much I hack away, nothing really seems to change.

But it was a great puzzling fun!  I really enjoyed it, and mean no disrespect.  It was just a different kind of experience.

A fraught day, waiting for test results, and I really wanted something cozy and fun to calm me down. TARTs and GLAZEDOVER donuts were not available, but found an It's-It in the freezer. 

The two of you may not always agree on clues, but the compromises are beautiful, and if there are battle scars, they don't show.  It's like watching a dance duo—I see you as the Rogers and Astaire of the puzzle world.

News never came, so the puzzle was doubly appreciated.  Thank you!  On to tomorrow.
Crunchy. But needs a Q.
Camano Island is a magical place. May your children always encounter such loving neighbors.

I thought it was Thursday puzzle drop. But glad I made the mistake. This was a fun puzzle.  Wednesday light?  But solid theme. I haven’t done an early week puzzle in so long.  I’m cheating myself. 

Not a big deal. But curious why 22 down. “Lemon add-on”. ADE.  didn’t have a question mark. I feel like it should have?  Not that the answer wasn’t obvious.  Just curious.  

I am going to end up down a rabbit hole with that GMO branding on 6 across. I had no idea. 


And I just did last Thursday. So I have to ask, how does that qualify for a Thursday?  I’m not speaking to difficulty. I’m speaking to what a Thursday is/isn’t. It wasn’t a trick. It was more a theme. 

To refresh. It was
Bro?  BROADWAY OPENING
Sit?  VISITOR CENTER
Ale?  FAIRY TAKE ENDING. 

Don’t get it twisted. I enjoyed the puzzle. It was fun. It just seemed more a theme than a trick. 

Thanks for your consideration.
When will the standard answer to “World Cup chant” be changed from OLE to “row”? ;-)
Me? I’m breaking up.

Perplexed by the combination of 10D, 16E, and 19E. Having never heard of TAEkwondo BOxing, Tori AMOS, and the deity ENOS, the fact I thought 10A was a bit of a joke and so answered with SOCK did me absolutely no favors! Other than these two letters, a nice puzzle. All the nay-sayers can stick to Friday, which I will gladly continue to (attempt to) solve in Easy Mode :)

I enjoyed this puzzle, as I always enjoy puzzles by Zhou and Kevin. 

But I was really touched by their story. What a rare gift, in today’s increasingly isolated society, to gain such a friend as a result of accidental proximity. I am so sorry for your loss, and for Kaylin’s loss as well. 

Happy birthday, Kae. May your life always be as cozy and joyous as a a big blanket taco.

Nice Wednesday puzzle. Not easy, not difficult. Just enough thinking for the middle of the week. Plus a fun little theme at that! 

Well done to the creator. This puzzle left me well satisfied!

GMO and GE are different things. Almost everything we eat is a GMO. Pink glows and neon fish are GEs. Don’t spread disinformation. 
Sincerely, a geneticist.

ROGER Roger, I COPY, OVER and OUT. 

Really enjoyed this one!

So very sorry to read about the loss of your dear neighbor and friend. And I woke up sad this morning. Not common for me. But here’s a little (one minute) walkie-talkie chuckle from comedian Brian Regan- https://youtu.be/MdHS8rQ0KLg?is=dGDws1XtsaE0zDTL Have a lovely day all.
Perfect theme echo in HAM (as in operator).

Zhou and Kevin make puzzles, as they say in their notes, to spark joy. I get joy just seeing Zhou’s photo in XwordInfo, and so will you: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Author/Zhou_Zhang . This was a very clever idea for a theme, and so entertaining because it’s centered on wordplay and humor, on imagining these wacky conversations. High props for originality. Did I enjoy it? Affirmative! Two serendipitous subthemes stood out: • Food! – ATE, OMNIVORE, GOOD EATS, OAT, TART, CHICKEN, PONZU, PICANTE, HAM, OLIVE, FAVA, PECAN, ADE. • Schwa enders! – TOGA, AGORA, FAVA, RAMONA, PEPA, ZARA, MAGNA, PAJAMA. I also liked seeing LEAP, which echoed Monday’s TRIPLE JUMP puzzle. Zhou and Kevin, I was won over by your fun theme; my brain was also happified by tangling with some sticking points. Your puzzles have a buoyant energy that launch me smiling into the day. Thank you for that, and for doing it once more today!

The southwest corner was a bit tricky, but thanks to Brooklyn 99's mention of PICANTE beef, and IMPs I was able to figure out it wasn't rev (up) but rather PEP. Couldn't remember the last name NESS but APPRISES seemed like to most logical combination of letters, even if I haven't seen that word before in my life.

Fun puzzle, I really enjoyed the theme. The answers came out naturally once I reached the revealer.
CARBONCOPY made me laugh, very clever theme

poem made from words in today’s puzzle
a/ i see you, son see you come up the road you look ready for things for tomorrow’s symphony d/ for the rainy world for a different world that’s not the one a/ i made up

Where is the printable puzzle?  We don't play online and seem to often have problems printing the large print Sunday version and the daily play only online.  As old folks in our 80'sand 90's we look forward to playing online and a printable puzzle.

Here's Tori doing Paul--twice, both live: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NjI3ots9m70&list=RDNjI3ots9m70&start_radio=1 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dfXsDQxmXcI&list=RDdfXsDQxmXcI&start_radio=1 (Who needs a back-up band when you've got a Bösendorfer?)

Random thought re today's headline.

Sam, do you know who Art Linkletter is?

Almost an ideal Wednesday puzzle. The theme was clever and expertly handled. The fill was interesting, and the level of challenge seemed quite appropriate. Took me more than twenty minutes to complete this one.

Didn't know PONZU or RAMONA, but the crosses assisted and the N at the intersection seemed obvious.

"What's the biochemistry exam about again?" might have been a tad better than the clue that appeared, but that's a very minor quibble.

Very fine Wednesday puzzle. Thanks!
Wonderful clever puzzle, a delight to solve.  THANK YOU
Great puzzle! Really nice way to convert a random vernacular into a crossword theme

I was so surprised to see Sam use the term "dungarees." I haven't heard that since I was a teenager in Brooklyn. Everyone used to buy their dungarees at The Dungaree Factory, where each pair had the weight of the thickest ones sold today. And boy, were they stiff until they were washed 50 times and got broken in.  I think they even hemmed them for free. And no one ever called them jeans. That was a long time ago.

There was a huge PRBUZZ when cotton candy first came out.
Some medical experts were even led to believe that this kind of sugar was healthier for you - but those were just the spun doctors.

A theme based on walkie-talkies, my favourite childhood fun? TOGA URL, good buddies!

I found this moderately challenging (finished around 15% under my average time) and quite an enjoyable way to start my Wednesday, having coffee, telling my dog to stop barking at the squirrels, chipmunks and birds who only want their breakfast at our various feeders, and listening to Art Blakey’s Paris Jam Session. 

It was only when I started writing my comment that I went back and read the Gameplay column, and the constructors’ note. I am so sorry for your loss, truly. I believe we’re supposed to make the world a better place by our being in it and it seems your neighbor Isa did that. May you and their partner find solace in your grief.
I’m so sorry about Isa. They sound amazing; what a loss. And still there is room today to celebrate 10 years old for Kaela! Thank you for the puzzle and the peek into your lives.
A perfect mid-week puzzle.  Well done.

TOGAs were only for special occasions; TUNICs were the daily wear...
PIN UP?  As a long-time user of Bobby-Pins, I must say that I've never encountered this phrase in relation to a fashionable "do." I'm from the era of Toni Home Permanents and hairspray.  We graduated into gel and rollers in the early 60's, while some people ironed their curly hair to try and straighten it.  Then there were the hot rollers.  Now it's mousse and a curling iron...and maybe hairspray if it's raining.  Haven't used a bobby-pin in...oh, 50 years or more.  

The WALKIE-TALKIE puzzle is a very entertaining and ingenious piece of work.  Obviously it leads some solvers off on tangents--either along Memory Lane or up the creek or down a garden path.  

The IMPs were never really pesky; it was the do-nothing disrupters that annoyed.  My secret tactic was to hook them into being interested...ha ha! Caught ya looking!

I've disliked so many recent puzzles that I'm compelled to express how much I enjoyed this one.

Like many others, apparently, I was immediately optimistic when I saw it was a Curry/Zhang collaboration. It did not disappoint. Clever clueing, reasonable trivia, fun theme. Every older Gen X-er remembers CB's - it was nice to reminisce for a minute.

For me, it was a perfect Wednesday puzzle, and with a nice cup of coffee, a really wonderful start to the day. 

Thank you both!

Much harder for me than figuring out why the CHICKEN crosses the road was figuring out why Santa says ROGER.  JOLLY, yes, but ROGER, no.  What am I missing about Santa?

Yes, a doughnut can be GLAZED OVER.

Would a CARBON COPY be discussed in a chemistry class?  No, but plain old CARBON would be.

When a CHICKEN OUT turns out not to be the animal that crosses the road, the scales fall from my eyes.  These are one-word replies to questions, followed by WALKIE-TALKIE radio words.

Aha.  Cute, once you get it.  A little bit weird before you get it.

I found this hard in the RPG, GLAZED OVER, ZARA section.  And PINUP was a mystery before crosses:  Can PINUP be defined as a "hairstyle", retro or not?  Don't you PIN UP your hair to get to a hairstyle, such as an updo?  Never have I heard anyone say "I wear a PINUP."  Over to you, as they say in the WALKIE-TALKIE biz.
It kind of made me feel smart and dumb at the same time.  Great puzzle!

I think it's a great sign when I have to double-check myself more than a few times but each pass untangles more of the grid. Somehow the bottom left corner was my biggest obstacle but the clues wound up being as helpful as they were pretty funny.

"Pinkglow pineapples and GloFish, for short." has to be the most confusing clue I've ever seen. Are we looking at the menu of the fancy smoothie bar? I think I'm better off not knowing what these nightmarish mutant creatures might be, and sticking to meat and potatoes.

Nice Wednesday puzzle. As an army veteran this one was right up my alley. Still took me a bit to catch on, but then it all filled fairly smoothly. And of course a puzzle find. A Monday from October 2, 2017 by Trenton Charlson. Some theme clues and answers: "A B C D E F G" MUSICNOTES "A B C D F" LETTERGRADES "B C F H I K N O P S U V W Y" CHEMICALSYMBOLS "G R X" MOVIERATINGS "A B O" BLOODTYPES Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/2/2017&h=39a ....

Fun and wonderful theme.

Dear Kevin and Zhou, your notes saddened and inspired me. Thank you for sharing so much of yourselves.
Delightful theme! I found myself smiling as I filled.

So those posters were about the *hair*?
That message did not come through loud or clear.
I never read the write up about the puzzle to avoid bias and rarely look at others comments - FABULOUS puzzle thanks guys! 5 star

What was the shout out in the clues? I can't find it.

Bossy is not a pun.

Not my favorite puzzle today.  Very hard by Wednesday standards and not in a way that feels rewarding when you solve it.  A lot of proper nouns, niche brand names and answers that only 70% made sense when you got them.
What "italicized clues"?

Felt just right for a Wednesday, enjoyable theme, grid not too easy but a bit breezy. Felt delightful overall, maybe I was just won over by 1D (both the idea of pajama pants and a rainy day - it’s so hot here).
Really satisfying and fun puzzle today. Loved every second of it! Hope everyone has a great Wednesday

Outside of the SE corner of the puzzle was a breeze… 

Loved the theme! 

Rating:
9/10

It's always a pleasure to see a puzzle from these two, and today was no exception. 
What a beautiful tribute in the constructor notes. Isa sounds like a wonderful neighbor and person, and I'm sorry for your loss.

I was so sure the graverobber would be either Burke or Hare. 
Such a great crossword today.
Laughter and tears. Thank you. 🙏🏼

In about 1991 our long distance phone charges where I work were substantial.

We had access to cheap calls on the FTS or Federal Telecommunications System which had so much static it was often useless.

PCs not yet ubiquitous, and some new Digital Equipment Corporation Ultrix/UNIX workstations shipped with audio point-to-power internet phone call system, if the other person also had the same workstation.

Used it for a while but the lag and dropped calls were so prominent we had to implement OVER and ROGER.  Gave up in a few weeks.

We also had a leased line for dedicated data transfer, costing $10’s of thousands a year, if my memory is correct.  Not the internet.  A line that somehow we had exclusive access to, to a central facility near Chicago.  One holy grail was we thought the US power grid had laid optical fiber throughout the entire US but we never figured out how to get into it.  The pre-internet universe.

Seems a different universe compared to now.
That was clever and SO FUN!!!!!  Great hump day puzzle!
A wonderful and joyful tribute to your friend. Well done.

This Father's Day week this puzzle reminds me of my father who had a ham radio  "shack" forever....

When I was away at college, he often suggested that I find a ham radio operator who would let me "patch" into him, but I never took him up on it. The phone seemed a better option! Probably I should have.

Dad had to learn Morse Code, but I believe now it is no longer a requirement.
This was so fun! I set a Wednesday record with today’s puzzle!

Another late puzzle find. The "link letters" clue led me to this one. Anyway - A Monday from January 31, 1994 by Janet R. Bender. Three theme answers in that one: ARTLINKLETTER FREDCOUPLES CHARLIEJOINER Thought that was kind of clever. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/31/1994&h=20a ....
What’s your vector victor?

As per usual, I completed the puzzle without clueing into the theme. It is only when I wander over here and then the lightbulb comes on. Nice doable puzzle today. Slightly better than average. Thanks for your talents constructors!
The theme rewarded a little thought but didn't beat anybody over the head. Nicely constructed and really felt like a Wednesday, whatever that means.
One nitpick: I feel like PICANTE should have been clued as "/Muy/ spicy" — at least something to indicate that the answer was in Spanish.

Dear constructors of NYT Crossword Puzzles... Please stop trying to make "at sea" and "asea" happen.  No one talks like that (anymore anyway).  
LOL!
I loved this one - had to do some minimal checking of names and trivia but I clued into the theme and had a good chuckle. Thank you :)
Lovely. Soothing to solve after a stressful day.

Question for the regular puzzlers. The comments section is already "closed" for me on yesterday's puzzle. Is this a new protocol or an anomaly? It seems like this comment section also pops up quite differently as well. 🙏🏼 Thanks for your insight!

I didn’t vibe with a lot of the clues but once I figured out the theme, it fell into place. 

Chicken OUT!!
@zhousephine What a wonderful cause! All of our pets are from shelters. They've brought so much to our lives. ❤️

@zhousephine

Are you going to let us in on the Easter egg clue?  
I’ll take your constructor note as literal, and assume the clue for 41A is for your girl?

I’m sorry for your loss. And really really sorry for Kaylin.

@Weak

Look at the starts of the first five down clues.
@zhousephine Bravo!
@Marshall Walthew how do you feel about hall and oates, cause they happen to squeeze in there too lol

@Dan

I thought about posting the same thing, but the more I thought about it, the more that I came to accept the clueing.

Often, that phrase is used in situations where the minimum magnitude is being questioned and the maximum magnitude is not really relevant.

Imagine, for example, a wife gets a promotion at work, and the wife doesn't think the husband is excited for her. The husband might say, "I'm no less excited about this than you are." No one would interpret that to mean that the husband is more excited than the wife."

On the other hand, there are situations where the phrase would in fact be used as a lower bound. Same couple goes to a party. The husband isn't thrilled about going. The wife says, "We need to be there for no less than an hour before we can leave." They can spend two hours there if they're both having a good time. The wife is not saying they will leave after exactly an hour

Steven M.— I make a sharp distinction between "at least as much as" and "more than". Because I know that when people say X is at least as much as Y, they often are not trying to say that X is more than Y.
@Mean Old Lady in your pajamas?

@Dan
As in, "And then they elected him President, no less!"
@Agent86 I suppose they are what you are supposed to wear with your Panama hat!

@Agent86
I had "neLLY ROGER" for a long time, thinking Santa called "Whoa, Nelly!" to his reindeer, and that the theme had something to do with two names. And it rains a lot in Panama, right?
Bob — it might be more fun to wear pajamas with a pajama hat.

@Bill and @Agent86

All y'all are so silly!
@john ezra I'm sure you could eat a KOI and an [32A] as well, as long as you add plenty of SALT-N-PEPA

@john ezra

And I'd probably skip the glofish and pinkglow pineapples.  Yuck.
@Chungclan I totally agree, didn’t even think of GMO food! Not available at my agora anyway.
@SP bras are good at lifting and covering

@shortbreadhead
Yes, and so fulfilling.

@shortbreadhead

I think SP got that.

@shortbreadhead

Yes I was commenting how clever that was

@Matt

I seem to recall one quiz in IB Chem being all about carbon. Including how carbon binds to certain atoms in certain ways and key aspects of fatty acid chains.

@Matt

Maybe they should have said organic chemistry, which basically is all about carbon.

@Matt

You got to it first!

I was coming here to say that there are approximately 20 million known chemical compounds, and approximately all of them contain carbon.

So, good luck!

@Francis, there are now close to 300 million compounds in the CAS (Chemical Abstracts Service) registry, and those are only those compounds that have been published in journals, patents, and the like. Some chemical vendors now offer catalogs with over 100 billion compounds (mostly synthesized on demand).

@Matt
If you think that's impossible, let me tell you a story about my mother, who was a chemistry major. Preparing for the comprehensive exams, she reviewed the archive of previous ones and sussed out that the up coming one was very likely to be all about nitrogen.  And she was right! Rewarded  with graduating magna cum laude AND phi beta kappa. Well those weren't just from that but from 4 years of hard work. She was a scholarship student in the depression and the child of divorce with 3 younger siblings and a lot of aunts and uncles who thought she should get a nice job with Aetna Insurance and help her mother. Shout out therefore to my grandmother who would have none of that. All of those siblings got to college too. And grandmother spent her later years learning to play the organ and classical guitar. I have some ancestors to live up to!

@Matt
Whatever the noble gases are, C is the opposite. It'll snuggle up to loads of other chemicals in the friendliest fashion, offering to play ring-around-the-rosey. And yet it's not like fluorine that, if I remember my chem correctly, will hug you so hard it'll break your arms!
Even if I weren't a carbonaceous organism, I think I'd like carbon.

PS Forgot to point out your hometown's appearance lately in the grid. Whoo!
@Matt Have you heard of carbohydrates?

@SBK in TO,

I too saw the mention of his “stomping grounds”  a few days ago. Didn’t know if anyone had mentioned it.

@Andrew

Well there’s KOS—and there’s a triangle with the letters KAE
@Andrew perhaps it’s “you’ve got a lovely daughter”?

@Andrew

I sort of thought it might have been meant only for her.
@Andrew   It's in the clues, spelled out.

@Keith

Good find!
@Andrew, as mentioned earlier, it's the first letters of the first 5 down clues.

@Don H,
 
At first I thought it was 44A, with the 10 spelled backwards after her “K” initial.
@Kyle Ha! Good one! But we did get (Paul) SIMON crossing MRS. (Robinson)! ;-) https://youtu.be/CwIlYKbqIPg?is=Dycn0thoW8m_VtV-

@Strudel Dad Glad you enjoyed the link! And I appreciate that you included Garfunkel who, in my opinion, was the one with the exquisite voice. You prompted me to go down the rabbit hole with Dustin Hoffman. I wasn't aware of "Tuner" and now will wait for it to stream in one of the services I have. Shocking as it is that Mr. Hoffman is already 88 years old, I find it fabulous that he is healthy and still acting. I never watched "Death of a Salesman" — neither the play nor the movie — but, rather, read it in high-school. I'm a huge Arthur Miller fan ever since reading "The Crucible" also in high-school. "Death of a Salesman" laid bare the pathos of the American Dream, something I couldn't relate to at the time because I was living in Brazil. That's probably why "The Crucible" had a greater impact on me, as Brazil was then a dictatorship so that there were parallels to be drawn between McCarthyism, the Salem witch-hunts, and dictatorships. Your post prompted me to go down the Miller rabbit hole as well, and I found this article I entirely missed whilst reading The Guardian: https://www.theguardian.com/culture/2026/may/07/arthur-miller-opens-up-about-marriage-to-marilyn-monroe-in-newly-unearthed-recordings (Despite the title, it's not just about his marriage to MM.) Thank you for replying to my post. The ensuing rabbit holes have been edifying and made my day a little richer!

@Francis
Than you, Francis.  Fingers crossed.
@dutchiris hoping for good news today and thinking of you during the long wait
@kkseattle I've seen other people use the term "crunchy." What does that mean, in the context of a crossword puzzle? And what's a Q?

@SP I'll wait for their answer. I remember that I asked what crunchy meant before and you answered and  indicated that you weren't sure. So I'm hoping the person who used those terms would be able to fill me in.

@Mean Old Lady

Which you can add oyster crackers to make a bit crunchy.
@Weak as I recall (without going back to check), I think that one used the sequential placement of the theme words as part of the gimmick, giving the solve more than one dimension.

@Weak
If you read last Thursday's comments, you would have seen many had the same opinion.

@Weak

You may want to go back to early week puzzles. They’ve gotten a little bit chewier lately, and you never know what clever theme you might uncover.

@Weak

That was pretty weak sauce (no offense) for a Thursday last week, gotta agree. Which is a shame because they would be perfectly decent puzzles on the right day, nothing against it.

@Martin S For the benefit of the two or three people who haven't been following Norway's successes at the World Cup: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/WjJ95MxI6_4 https://www.youtube.com/shorts/BjqUpRrHVo8

@The X-Phile
Is that ROW with "OW as in OWIE" or "OW as in OH!"?

I'm just too busy with projects and activities to follow soccer games.  I know about Christiano and Lionel only for XWord purposes...and they were on the news.  I am happy that everyone is having such a good time.  
But I am using my spare time to worry about other stuff.

@Pat-a-pon 
Enos is a biblical character, the son of Seth who was the son of Adam. Of course, that doesn't help if you never heard of him, either!

@Francis You might be pleased to know that the kids these days have no problem with it. My teenager always wears socks with slides and informs me that it is FAR more humiliating to show your toes in public.

@NanuNanu
And Enos would not be considered a deity.

@NanuNanu Interestingly enough, as a certified Catholic (literally I have a certificate) and as a person who reads the Bible daily, I have just had to add this one to my 'crosswordese' dictionary as I hadn't heard of him either.

@Calig You have to remember that accuracy is not important to either the constructors or the editors.

Whenever I encounter a clue that appears to involve an area I have specific knowledge about, I ask myself “how would someone ignorant of this topic answer?”

@Chet
One person's ignorance is another person's open-mindedness.

@Calig
G**gle sez:
GMO vs. GEO
While often used interchangeably in popular media, scientific and regulatory bodies differentiate them:GMO (Genetically Modified Organism): A broad term that can describe any organism whose genes have been altered, including via traditional selective breeding or modern genetic engineering.GEO (Genetically Engineered Organism): A more specific term reserved for organisms modified using modern molecular biology techniques, such as recombinant DNA (transgenics) or gene-editing tools like CRISPR.

A nit not worth picking imo.

@Calig

What you’re missing is how crossword clues work. If practically everything we eat is a GMO, and two GEOs fall into this broad category, then they are GMOs, and the clue is good.

@Chet
Accuracy is important to the people behind the puzzles. What do you think all those editors and reviewers do all day?! No need to insult them.
Besides, a clue is NOT a definition. It is a set of words designed to lead you to the answer, by whatever devious path will serve. The clue's 'failure' to distinguish genetic engineering from all other means of genetic modification is not inaccurate.
All other prime numbers are odd but it's not wrong to call 2 a prime number for clueing purposes.

@Charles Peterson I remember discussing this generally when GEOs were new in a horticulture class. It is astonishing how much people have changed plants, thousands of years before we could attempt genetic engineering. Heirloom plants are not exactly natural nor are they always better.  Somebody selected, traded, rogued, or otherwise chose one form or another. Then we hybridized and grafted and cell-cultured plants.

Some claim (tongue-in-cheek) that humans exist to change plants. The most changed plant is probably the vast orchid family. Perhaps some all powerful being is admiring our work.

@Lewis
Yes, this is very good for people to understand, especially new constructors. Rejections don’t mean it’s a bad puzzle. The competition is fierce out there, and sometimes puzzles can be rejected just because the theme just didn’t resonate, or because they are looking for a certain variety, or maybe they’ve just accepted a similar one. And don’t assume you have an in just because you’ve published before. If Zhou and Kevin have such a low acceptance rate, just imagine!
